wikitext:
{{Chess Opening Theory/Position |Mikenas defence |eco=[[Chess/ECOA|A40]] |parent=[[../|Queen's pawn opening]] |aka=Bogoljubov–Mikenas defence, Lundin defence, Nimzowitsch queen's pawn}} ==1...Nc6 · Mikenas defence == A rare move. 1...Nc6 develops a knight and prepares ...e5. Part of the idea is, like a reflection of the Alekhine defence, 1. e4 Nf6, to encourage White to overextend in the centre before counter-attacking. Original positions can arise if White plays d5, kicking the knight, either immediately, [[/2. d5|'''2. d5''']], or after a sequence like [[/2. c4|'''2. c4''']] e5 3. d5. Otherwise the often game transposes into positions more commonly reached by other move orders. [[/2. Nf3|'''2. Nf3''']] is most common move, developing and controlling e5. This allows Black to play 2...d5 and the game resumes some sense of normality, although it is unusual in 1. d4 d5 games for Black to play Nc6 so early and deprive themselves of the option to move their c-pawn. [[/2. c4|'''2. c4''']] d5 transposes into the Chigorin defence to the Queen's gambit, normally seen in the order 1. d4 d5 2. c4 Nc6. [[/2. e4|'''2. e4''']] transposes into the Nimzowitsch defence, usually seen after 1. e4 Nc6 2. d4. ==Theory table== {{Chess Opening Theory/Table}} '''1.d4 Nc6'''
